source.title: Function of the thalamic reticular complex: the searchlight hypothesis
source.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
source.notes: Original plan cited PMID 6584887 (incorrect — resolves to unrelated rat thymosin paper). Correct PMID is 6589612 (PNAS 81(14):4586–4590, 1984). Confirmed via PubMed.

confidence_rationale: The TRN push-pull gating mechanism is the most direct biological instantiation of the sensorium gate in the three-gate model. The TRN selects which sensory/thalamic channel gains access to cortical processing — this is precisely the sensorium loop's function. Malsburg synapse / transient assembly concept grounds the thought loop's rapidly reconfigurable binding requirement. Moderate transfer risk from 1984 theory paper (predates modern BG-thalamic understanding and oscillatory frameworks).

mapping.source_claim_statement: The thalamic reticular nucleus (TRN) controls selective attention by producing burst firing in a targeted subset of thalamic relay neurons while inhibiting others via GABAergic suppression. The TRN is positioned to receive collateral input from both thalamocortical and corticothalamic pathways and implements push-pull gating: attended channel amplified, unattended channels suppressed. Rapidly modifiable Malsburg synapses create transient cell assemblies ('vertical assemblies' uniting neurons across cortical hierarchy levels).
mapping.ree_translation: TRN = physical substrate of the sensorium gate; TRN burst = mechanism by which the gate selects which sensory/environmental stream reaches E3 attention. Push-pull structure = mutual exclusion between attended (sensorium loop active) and unattended channels. Malsburg synapses / transient vertical assemblies = mechanism for rapidly reconfigurable binding in the thought loop — E1 predictions, E3 evaluations, and affective weightings unified into candidate trajectories via burst-driven transient assemblies.
mapping.mapping_caveat: 1984 paper predates modern understanding of BG-thalamic circuit interactions, beta oscillations, and loop-specific gating. The searchlight specifically addresses sensory thalamic channels; extension to non-sensory thalamic nuclei (MD, VA, VL serving cognitive loops) requires additional claims not made in this paper. The Malsburg synapse concept has been partially superseded by modern plasticity frameworks.
mapping.source_context: PNAS, 1984. Foundational theoretical paper proposing TRN as attentional searchlight controller.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Crick, foundational, widely cited). High mapping fidelity for sensorium gate. Moderate-high transfer risk given age of paper and conceptual gaps to modern BG/oscillatory frameworks.
